key_departures.impact: Parham was a homegrown three-year starter at guard, appearing in 64 games with 63 starts. His departure leaves a hole at interior guard depth, though the Raiders were already planning to upgrade the position via Linderbaum and potentially the draft.
Cappa played 17 games for the Raiders with 8 starts at RG/C. Released as a cap casualty. With Linderbaum signed and Jackson Powers-Johnson at RG, the release is a managed move. Minimal need impact.

Traded to the Jets along with a 7th-round pick for a 6th-round pick. Smith was the bridge starter and his departure formalizes the QB need — though this was already expected given the Raiders hold the No. 1 overall pick and are expected to select Fernando Mendoza.
Backup QB acquired via trade last offseason. Departure leaves the backup QB role entirely vacant. Raiders still need a veteran backup/mentor for the incoming rookie QB.
Started 13 games filling in for an injured Kolton Miller at LT. The Raiders had no plans to re-sign him. With Miller returning healthy, this is a non-factor at LT. The backup OT depth is thinner.
Johnson played just 9 games in 2025 after a broken fibula in training camp. Was not a priority for the Raiders. Minimal impact on safety need picture.
Adams was not a high-priority player for Las Vegas. Depth loss only. No meaningful impact on team needs.
The new regime decided to move on from the veteran kicker. The Raiders replaced him with Matt Gay in free agency.

signings.analysis: Record-breaking deal for the highest-paid interior lineman in NFL history. The contract is effectively fully guaranteed — $60M fully guaranteed at signing with the 2028 salary ($21M) becoming guaranteed the following March. Three-time Pro Bowler, 96.2% pass block win rate since 2022, has missed only 2 games in 4 seasons. This is the centerpiece move of the entire offseason. IOL need (specifically center) is fully resolved. Linderbaum also clarifies JPJ's role as starting RG. Still a need at guard depth/left guard, but center is DONE for the foreseeable future.

signings.trade_details: Raiders sent 6th-round pick (#182 overall) to Bills; received Johnson and 7th-round pick (#228 overall)
signings.analysis: Acquired via trade on March 8. Johnson is a former All-Pro (2023) nickel/slot CB with 113 career games. However, this is only a 1-year remaining deal at $8.67M cap hit — a bridge/prove-it carry on his existing Bills contract, not a new commitment. He partially addresses the slot/nickel CB need but the Raiders still need a longer-term CB solution. He pairs with Eric Stokes (re-signed) to give the team two above-depth starting CBs, but the commitment structure keeps this a 'partially addressed' grade.

Re-signed on a 3-year, $30M deal with $20M guaranteed. Stokes started a career-high 16 games in his first season with the Raiders. The 3-year term with $20M in guarantees shows real investment in keeping him as a starter. Combined with Taron Johnson, the Raiders now have two committed starting CBs. CB need is substantially reduced but not fully addressed — they lack elite CB1 talent and still lack depth behind Stokes/Johnson.

3-year, $48M deal with $31.28M guaranteed (per Spotrac). Paye is a former 1st-round pick (21st overall, 2021) who started 74-of-75 games for the Colts. He has 30.5 career sacks but was down to 4 sacks in 2025 — a career low. His AAV of $16M is mid-tier for EDGE. Critically, the failed Maxx Crosby trade means Crosby is still on the Raiders roster. So the EDGE room now features Crosby + Paye + Koonce — an above-average trio. EDGE need is partially addressed by the Paye signing alone, but with Crosby's unexpected return, the need is effectively resolved for 2026. However, Crosby's future is uncertain (knee concerns, trade request history), so the need remains on the horizon.

Re-signed on a 1-year, $11M deal. Koonce posted 4.5 sacks and 13 QB hits in 17 games in 2025. He has a relationship with DC Rob Leonard and fits the 3-4 scheme. The 1-year structure means this is a prove-it deal — depth/rotational designation but high-upside. Adds further depth to the EDGE room alongside Crosby and Paye. Need unchanged on its own, but collectively the EDGE room is in good shape for 2026.

3-year, $36.03M deal with $23M guaranteed (per Spotrac). Dean is 25 years old, a Super Bowl champion with Philadelphia, and one of the best blitzing LBs in the NFL. He posted a 40.7% QB pressure rate in 2025 — highest on the Eagles. Injury history (has missed 28 of 68 possible games) is the caveat, but the 3-year commitment with meaningful guarantees signals the Raiders view him as a true starter. Paired with Quay Walker, the Raiders have completely overhauled their linebacker corps. LB need fully addressed.

3-year, $40.5M deal with $28M guaranteed (per Spotrac). Walker led the Packers in tackles for all four of his NFL seasons. Career-high 128 tackles in 2025. One of only three players since 2000 with 100+ tackles and 1.5+ sacks in each of his first four seasons. The Raiders specifically targeted missed tackles as a major issue in 2025 — Walker comes with a 4.5% missed tackle rate. With Dean, the Raiders now have arguably the best young LB pairing they've had in over a decade. LB is fully addressed.

3-year, $35.03M deal with $23M guaranteed (per Spotrac/OTC). Nailor averaged 15.4 yards per reception in 4 seasons with the Vikings, including career highs of 29 catches, 444 yards, and 4 TDs in 2025. He is a Las Vegas native (Bishop Gorman HS) and projects as a slot/WR2 with starter upside. However, critics correctly note Nailor is a No. 3-caliber receiver stepping into a No. 2 role. His AAV of $11.7M is mid-tier for WR. He partially addresses the WR need — gives the Raiders a legitimate starting option for Mendoza — but does NOT solve the lack of a true WR1. WR remains a need for the draft.

2-year deal, low guaranteed money (per reports of minimal guarantees). Heyward is a versatile FB/TE/ST player — 68 career games, 44 career receptions. Kubiak specifically wants a fullback in his offense. This is a role-player/special teams signing. Brock Bowers remains a top-3 TE in the NFL. TE is not a need, and this signing doesn't change that picture.

1-year deal at minimum/near-minimum value (per Spotrac cap data showing $1.6M). Young is primarily a special teams/kick returner — averaged 32.2 yards per kick return in Seattle in 2025. Won a Super Bowl with HC Klint Kubiak in Seattle. Depth/ST signing that adds zero to the WR starter need picture.

1-year deal. Replaces released Daniel Carlson. Gay split time between Washington and San Francisco in 2025, going 17-of-23 on FGs (73.9%). Not a premium kicker, but fills the void left by Carlson's departure. K is resolved for 2026.

notable_non_signings_context.maxx_crosby_trade_saga: The Raiders agreed in principle on March 6 to trade Maxx Crosby to the Baltimore Ravens for two first-round picks (including the No. 14 pick in 2026). The Ravens backed out of the deal on March 11 due to medical concerns from Crosby's physical (torn meniscus repaired in January). Crosby confirmed he is remaining a Raider. The Raiders had already committed ~$281.5M in free agency in anticipation of freeing Crosby's $30M cap number. This cap reversion cost Las Vegas approximately $30M in projected cap relief, and they lost the No. 14 pick that was factored into their rebuild plan. Crosby remains on the roster at a ~$35.79M cap hit.
notable_non_signings_context.geno_smith_trade: Traded to the New York Jets along with a 7th-round pick for a 6th-round pick. Effectively ends the bridge QB era. No. 1 pick and Fernando Mendoza incoming.
notable_non_signings_context.maxx_crosby_status: Remains on roster with roughly $30M 2026 cap hit. Is committed to playing in Las Vegas per his public statements. Trade market significantly cooled by failed Ravens physical.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Tyler Linderbaum (3yr/$81M, $60M gtd) is the highest-paid interior lineman in NFL history and a three-time Pro Bowler. Center is resolved at an elite level for 3 years. Jackson Powers-Johnson is solidified at RG. Only remaining need is guard depth, especially at LG — can address in mid-rounds of the draft. IOL drops from critical to low-tier depth need.

Dual signing of Nakobe Dean (3yr/$36M, $23M gtd) and Quay Walker (3yr/$40.5M, $28M gtd) completely overhauls the LB corps with two 25-year-old Georgia products who have Super Bowl/high-production pedigrees. Both have multi-year real guarantees and are unquestioned starters. LB goes from a critical need to a luxury pick only. Dean's injury history is the only caveat.

Eric Stokes re-signed (3yr/$30M, $20M gtd) as a committed starter, and Taron Johnson acquired via trade provides quality slot/nickel coverage. Two solid starting CBs now committed. However, Johnson is on a legacy 1-year contract, CB2/CB3 depth remains thin, and neither Stokes nor Johnson is an elite CB1. CB drops one tier — still worth addressing in rounds 2-4 of the draft, but no longer an acute need.

The failed Maxx Crosby trade means Crosby (arguably the best EDGE in football when healthy) unexpectedly remains on the roster alongside newly signed Kwity Paye (3yr/$48M, $31.28M gtd) and re-signed Malcolm Koonce (1yr/$11M). The EDGE room is now above-average for 2026. However, Crosby's knee concerns and ongoing trade request history mean this need must be monitored — he is not a locked-in long-term piece. EDGE drops from critical to a depth/developmental need in this draft, with the understanding that Crosby could still be moved later.

Jalen Nailor (3yr/$35M, $23M gtd) provides a legitimate WR2-caliber option and starter for the offense, stepping into a primary role from a slot role with the Vikings. He partially addresses the WR need. However, Nailor is not an elite WR1 — he lacks the alpha receiver traits needed to be the top target for a rookie QB in Fernando Mendoza. WR remains a significant draft need, dropping one tier from critical to high priority. The Raiders still need a true No. 1 WR, ideally in rounds 1-2.

The Raiders hold the No. 1 overall pick and are widely expected to select Fernando Mendoza (Indiana). Geno Smith was traded away; Kenny Pickett departed to Carolina. There is zero experienced QB on the roster. The Raiders are actively seeking a veteran backup/mentor (Kirk Cousins reported as a target). QB remains the No. 1 draft need and pick; the veteran backup role is a free agency need that persists.

Kolton Miller (LT) is expected back healthy after Stone Forsythe filled in for 13 games in 2025. The departure of Forsythe is not impactful. However, RT remains a question mark — DJ Glaze struggled in 2025, and Charles Grant (2025 3rd-round pick) is the primary internal candidate. No OT was signed in free agency. OT remains a draft target, particularly at RT, unchanged.
No IDL signings were made. Thomas Booker was re-signed at depth (per reports). The Raiders need interior DL depth and a true nose tackle for the 3-4 scheme. Unchanged from pre-FA assessment.

Lonnie Johnson Jr. departed to Miami but was not considered a key piece. No safety was signed as a clear starter. The Raiders' safety room is unresolved. Unchanged from pre-FA assessment — still a priority for the draft.
Ashton Jeanty was drafted in 2025 and is a franchise RB. No changes needed. Depth RB Connor Heyward (versatile FB/TE) adds in a FB role. RB is not a need.

key_additions.headline: Record-breaking center deal — highest-paid interior lineman in NFL history anchors O-line for Mendoza era
Led Packers in tackles all four seasons; teams with Nakobe Dean to rebuild the Raiders' second level
Super Bowl champion and elite blitzing LB reunites with college teammate Walker to form the Raiders' new LB core
Former 1st-round pick (30.5 career sacks) joins Maxx Crosby to form a formidable pass-rush duo
Former first-round pick re-signed as committed starter after career-high 16 starts in first Raiders season
Former All-Pro slot CB acquired via trade (6th/7th swap) to strengthen Raiders' banged-up secondary
Las Vegas native returns home to serve as primary WR target for expected No. 1 pick Fernando Mendoza
Homegrown pass rusher re-signed on 1-year prove-it deal to complete three-headed EDGE rotation
Versatile fullback/TE signed to fill a Kubiak offense staple role on offense and special teams
Veteran kicker replaces released Daniel Carlson under new regime
Super Bowl-winning return specialist (32.2 yds/return in 2025) adds special teams depth under Kubiak

key_losses.headline: Bridge QB traded away, officially clearing the path to select Fernando Mendoza No. 1 overall
Homegrown guard starter (63 starts in 4 seasons) departs, leaving a hole at LG that Linderbaum's arrival can't fully fix
Backup QB departs, leaving the Raiders without an experienced QB behind incoming rookie Mendoza
Veteran guard released as cap casualty, part of broader IOL overhaul led by Linderbaum signing
Emergency LT starter (13 games for injured Kolton Miller) departs; Raiders expect Miller healthy in 2026
Long-tenured Raiders kicker not retained by new regime; replaced by Matt Gay

draft_capital_notes: Raiders hold 10 picks in 2026 draft. No. 1 overall pick expected to be used on QB Fernando Mendoza (Indiana). The Maxx Crosby trade with the Ravens was called off after Crosby's physical — the Raiders lost the anticipated No. 14 pick (from Baltimore) but retain their own No. 1. Raiders also hold compensatory 4th (No. 134) and 5th (No. 175) round picks. Draft priority positions post-FA: QB (No. 1), WR (Round 1-2), OT/IOL (Round 2-3), S (Round 3-4), CB depth (Round 3-5), IDL/nose tackle (Round 3-4).
